segments.s141-g25: ازان پس is a compound temporal adverb meaning “after that / from that time on.” بسازید is a 2nd-person-plural imperative (classical/formal) of ساختن/بسازیدن meaning “make/arrange/secure” and takes the following phrase as its definite direct object. سهراب را is the proper name marked by را, the definite object marker showing that Sohrab is the one to be acted upon.
segments.s141-g26: ببندید is another 2nd-person-plural imperative meaning “bind / tie up / secure.” یک شب is a duration phrase “one night” specifying how long; in poetic word order it follows the verb. برو is an imperative “go,” here used with خواب را (the sleep) to mean idiomatically “send/put (him) to sleep” or “let sleep come upon him.” خواب را is the definite object (marked by را) meaning “the sleep / sleep itself.”
segments.s141-g27: برفتند is a past plural verb (they went/they departed) placed before its subjects, a common poetic word order. بیدار is an adjective/adverbial ('awake') qualifying the subjects and follows the verb here to describe their state. دو پهلوان is the plural subject 'two champions/heroes' (number before noun).
segments.s141-g28: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/near' (به + نزدیک) marking direction/location; it precedes the personal name. سهراب is the proper name, and روشن‌روان is an epithet attached after the name ('bright‑souled'). Word order: preposition + adverbial + name + epithet.
segments.s141-g29: به پیش اندرون is a locative/directional phrase: به 'to' + پیش 'before / in the presence of' + اندرون 'inner [chambers]'; together it means 'into the inner presence' or 'before the inner court'. هدیه شهریار is a noun phrase 'the gift of the king', with the possessor (شهریار) following هدیه.
segments.s141-g30: ده اسپ و ده استر is a simple numeric phrase (number before noun) meaning 'ten stallions and ten mares', with و linking the two groups. The repeated به before زین and بار is an archaic/poetic marker meaning roughly 'with' or 'for' and attaches to each following noun (زین 'saddle', بار 'load') to indicate they come equipped 'with saddles and with baggage'.
segments.s141-g31: ز is the poetic/genitive preposition 'of/from' used to indicate material or provenance. پیروزه modifies تخت producing 'a throne of/inlaid with turquoise'; و links the two items; the second ز introduces the second item ('a crown of/from بیجاده'). This is a compact, paratactic listing typical of epic diction.
segments.s141-g32: This is a terse nominal string in which nouns and modifiers are stacked without linking particles: سر تاج زر reads as 'the crown's top/head (سر) — gold (زر)' i.e. the crown's top is gold, and پایه تخت عاج reads 'the throne's base/pedestal (پایه تخت) — ivory (عاج)'. The modifiers (زر, عاج) follow the heads to state material.
segments.s141-g41: به توران is a locative phrase ('to/into Turan' in epic sense = 'in/at Turan'). چو ... و چون ... is a simile construction: چو + name = 'like/as (someone)'; the repeated چو before each proper name is a poetic device linking both examples.
segments.s141-g42: دلیر و سپهبد form the nominal predicate ('brave and a spahbed/commander'). نبد is an archaic negative past-form meaning 'there was not' or 'did not exist' (so the clause says no one of that sort existed). بی‌گمان is an adverb modifying the whole clause ('undoubtedly').
segments.s141-g43: فرستادم is first‑person past ('I sent' / 'I have sent'). اینک = 'now' (temporal). به فرمان تو = 'by/at your command' where به+noun expresses the means/authority ('by your order').
segments.s141-g44: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'); باشند is 3rd person plural subjunctive/present after که and here means 'may be'/'will be' (they be). یک چند is an archaic quantifier meaning 'a few' / 'some'. مهمان تو = 'your guests' (مهمان as noun, تو = possessive 'your').
segments.s141-g45: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). جنگ جویی تو is an elliptic/poetic phrase meaning 'if you are a seeker of war' (جنگ + جویی 'seeking/pursuit' yields 'seeking war'); تو marks the person 'you'. جنگ آورند is a 3rd person plural verb 'they bring/cause war' (future or general present).
segments.s141-g46: جهان is 'the world' or 'life' as the object. بر بداندیش = 'against / upon the ill‑thinking (i.e. the malicious)'. تنگ آورند is a compound meaning 'they make (it) narrow' — idiomatically 'they make life hard / distress / oppress'. The verb آورند is again 3rd plural 'they bring/cause'.
segments.s141-g47: چنین modifies the following nouns 'such a ...'. نامه و خلعت شهریار = 'a letter and the king's robe' (خلعت شهریار = the robe/gift associated with the shah). The two nouns are conjoined by و.
segments.s141-g48: ببردند is past plural 'they took / they carried off'. با ساز expresses accompaniment 'with musical instruments / with music'. چندان سوار = 'so many horsemen' (چندان = 'so many/much'), i.e. they were accompanied by a large number of riders.
segments.s141-g1: خبر شد is an idiom meaning ‘it became known’ or ‘word came’; خبر (noun) is the theme and شد (past of شدن) marks the event. به نزدیک افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase giving the recipient/location — به + نزدیک = ‘to/near’, with افراسیاب as the proper-name object placed after the preposition.
segments.s141-g2: که introduces a reported clause ‘that …’. افگند is the past of افکندن ‘to throw/set (something)’, here ‘launched/placed’; سهراب is the agent placed before the verb (common in epic word order). کشتی بر آب uses بر meaning ‘on/upon’ and آب = ‘water’, so the verb governs the boat being set upon the water.
segments.s141-g3: هنوز is an adverb ‘still/yet’. از دهن بوی شیر is a genitive-like expression ‘the scent of milk from the mouth’ with دهن ‘mouth’ governing the source. آیدش is آید (present) plus enclitic ش (to him/its) meaning ‘it comes to him’ or more naturally ‘comes from him/comes out (of him)’ in this context.
segments.s141-g4: همی is a habitual/continuous marker here (‘still’). رای شمشیر و تیر is ‘the thought/plan (رای) of sword and arrow’ (شمشیر و تیر in a genitive/qualifying relation). آیدش repeats the present construction ‘comes to him’ — i.e. the idea or inclination toward arms comes upon him.
segments.s141-g5: زمین را uses را to mark the definite/affected object ‘the earth’. به خنجر is an instrumental/agent phrase ‘by/with daggers’ (به used as ‘by/with’), and بشوید is a poetic/old present/future-like form from شدن meaning ‘become washed/soaked’ — here idiomatically ‘the earth will be stained (with blood)’. همی again gives a continuing or emphatic sense.
segments.s141-g6: کنون is the adverb ‘now’. رزم is the object ‘battle/war’ and کاووس is the subject (Kavus). جوید is the present/habitual form of جستن ‘to seek’ (here ‘seeks’). همی again adds a continuous/epic present nuance.
segments.s141-g7: سپاه انجمن شد is literally ‘the army became an assembly’ i.e. ‘the army gathered/in council’ (سپاه subject, انجمن complement, شد = became/assembled). برو بر بسی is a compact verbal phrase: برو (go/went forth) + بر (against/on) + بسی (a poetic quantifier, here ‘many’/‘much’), so the sense is ‘marched against many’ or ‘went out against numerous foes’.
segments.s141-g8: نیاید is negative present/subjunctive ‘does not come’ or ‘does not appear’. همی is emphatic/continuous. یادش is یاد + enclitic ش ‘his memory/remembrance’, and از هر کسی literally ‘from every person’ — together meaning ‘his memory does not come to anyone,’ idiomatically ‘no one remembers him.’
segments.s141-g65: Structure: adverbial time (هنوز) + demonstrative phrase (آن زمان) + predicate phrase (گستهم خرد) + copula (بود). Here هنوز آن زمان frames the temporal setting 'still/at that time'; گستهم (a name or attributive term placed before the noun) + خرد form a nominal predicate 'Gostaham (was) of wisdom'; بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is typical of classical Persian: time phrase → subject/predicate → copula.
segments.s141-g66: This is a prepositional + participial comment linked to the previous predicate: به introduces the target/direction 'toward' (به خردی = 'toward/with regard to wisdom'); گراینده is a present‑participle/adjectival form 'inclining' or 'disposed to', coordinated with گرد 'to go about/active' by و; the final بود is the copula repeating past tense. So the whole segment qualifies the subject as 'inclined to wisdom and active/roving.'
segments.s141-g67: یکی is the indefinite/identifying particle 'one' or 'a certain'; خواهرش is the noun 'his sister' with the possessive clitic -ش; بود is the past copula 'was'. The words گرد و سوار follow the copula as predicate adjectives/nouns ('گرد' here functioning as an adjectival/epithet meaning roughly 'roving' or 'active', and سوار 'mounted/rider') describing that sister.
segments.s141-g68: A sequence of three coordinated adjectival epithets modifying the preceding noun phrase: بداندیش 'ill‑minded/ill‑disposed', گردنکش 'headstrong/rebellious', and نامدار 'renowned/famed', linked by و. There is no new verb; these are stacked predicate adjectives that complete the description.
segments.s141-g69: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; it introduces the temporal clause. The clause has subject سهراب, a locative/relational noun نزدیکی + noun complement دژ ('the nearness/vicinity of the fortress'), and the past verb رسید 'arrived/reached'. Word order: conjunction → subject → locative phrase → verb (typical poetic order).
segments.s141-g70: This is an independent clause: هجیر is the subject (proper name) followed by the epithet دلاور 'the valiant' in apposition; سپه ('host/army') is the definite object and marked by the object marker را; بدید is the past verb 'saw'. The object marker را signals that سپه is definite/specific.
segments.s141-g71: نشست here is a past intransitive verb 'sat/mounted' (classical usage often 'sat upon' = 'mounted'); از بر is a compound prepositional phrase 'from off/upon' indicating movement relative to a mount, followed by بادپای 'swift‑footed' (an epithet, here the steed). چو is the simile 'like/as' and گرد functions as the comparison noun 'a whirl/around' — so the clause means 'he mounted the swift steed and, like a whirlwind...'.
segments.s141-g72: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; دژ is its object 'the fortress'. رفت is the past verb 'went/rode off'; پویان is a participial adjective 'moving/active' qualifying the motion; به introduces the destination 'to' and دشت نبرد is the compound destination 'the plain of battle' (دشت governed by نبرد). The sequence gives 'he went from the fortress, moving, to the plain of battle.'
segments.s141-g105: به دژ در is a locative phrase meaning “at the fortress gate” (به + place + در ‘gate’). چو is the subordinating conjunction “when”; آگه شدند is the past plural of شدن with آگه ‘informed/aware’ (آگه شدن = “to be informed”). از هجیر uses از + proper name to indicate the topic/source of the news: “about Hojir.” Word order is poetic/inverted but functions as “When those at the fortress-gate became informed about Hojir.”
segments.s141-g106: که introduces the content clause “that …”; او را is the third-person pronoun with the Persian definite-object marker را (“him” as direct object). گرفتند is past plural “they seized/captured,” و links actions, and بردند اسیر is a verb plus predicate: بردند “they carried/took” with اسیر as the resultative/complement “captive.”
segments.s141-g107: خروش آمد is a common collocation where the noun خروش ‘uproar/clamor’ is followed by آمد ‘came’ to mean “a clamor arose.” و links the next phrase; ناله مرد و زن is a noun phrase “the lamentation/cry of men and women” (no ezafe is shown but the sequence reads naturally as possessive/attributive).
segments.s141-g108: که here gives the reason (“for/because”); کم شد is a verbal phrase meaning “became few/was diminished” (کم ‘few/little’ + شد ‘became’). هجیر is the subject (name) and اندر آن انجمن is a locative phrase “in that assembly.” The clause explains why people wailed: Hojir had been reduced/was gone from the gathering.
segments.s141-g17: This is a prepositional indirect-object construction: به + [object] + گفت. به introduces the addressee (“to the commander”), گردان لشکر is a genitive group “troop/division of the army,” and گفت is the past-tense verb “said/told.” The grammatical subject is omitted (typical in Persian) and is supplied from context.
segments.s141-g18: که introduces the content clause ‘that …’. این راز is the clause’s subject/object phrase “this secret.” باید is an impersonal modal ‘must/ought to,’ and the verb phrase ماند نهفت is a poetic inversion: ماند (remain) + نهفت (hidden/concealed) = “remain hidden” (modern Persian: باید این راز نهفته بماند).
segments.s141-g19: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning ‘when.’ اندر آرند is a poetic present/plural form ‘they turn/bring in’ (آرند = 3rd pl.), with اندر giving the sense ‘in/into’ or ‘toward.’ هر دو (both) + بروی (directional ‘to/at the face’) indicates that both turned their faces toward each other; the clause uses poetic word order and an older verb form.
segments.s141-g20: This is a simple verbal sentence with the name/title تهمتن as subject and بود as the past copula ‘was.’ بی‌گمان is an adverb ‘undoubtedly,’ and چاره‌جوی is an agentive noun/participle ‘seeker of a remedy/solution,’ functioning as the predicate complement: ‘Tahmten was, surely, a seeker of a means/solution.’
segments.s141-g21: پدر را uses را to topicalize/mark ‘father’ as the (grammatical) object of the subordinate clause. نباید is the impersonal modal ‘ought not / must not,’ and که + داند introduces the subordinate clause ‘that (he) knows.’ Word order is poetic: the object (پدر را) appears before the modal and clause, and the subject (پسر) appears after the verb (داند).
segments.s141-g22: که introduces the content clause ‘that …’. بندد is a classical/subjunctival 3rd-person-singular verb ‘binds/fastens,’ and دل و جان is a compound object ‘heart and life.’ به مهر پدر is a prepositional phrase ‘to/with the father’s affection’ specifying the object of attachment. The clause order is inverted for verse (modern order: پسر نباید که دل و جانش را به مهر پدر بندد).
segments.s141-g23: مگر here has a rhetorical/conditional sense (roughly ‘perhaps’/‘unless’/‘is it that…?’). کان is an archaic demonstrative/as-if particle attaching to the noun (classical usage = ‘that’/‘as it were’), دلاور is ‘brave man,’ and گو is a quotative/parenthetical ‘it is said’ or ‘so-called.’ سالخورد is a participial/adjectival form ‘becoming old/aged.’ Together they form an evocative phrase: ‘perhaps that brave man, it is said, when aged…’
segments.s141-g24: This clause uses شود + کشته to express the passive ‘will be killed / will become killed.’ بر دست + این شیرمرد means ‘by the hand of this champion’ (بر used here to mark the agent/instrument in an older idiom). The sequence is a typical Classical Persian passive construction: شود کشته بر دست X = ‘be slain by X.’
segments.s141-g57: This is a temporal clause headed by the conjunction چون meaning 'when/since' (here introducing a time: 'when'). جهانجوی is a personal designation (a noun phrase subject). نامه شاه is the direct object 'the king's letter' (نامه modified by شاه); خواند is a past tense verb 'read' and completes the clause ('when Jahān‑juy read the king's letter'). Word order is Subject–(temporal) conjunction + Object + Verb, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s141-g58: This is the main clause: ازان جایگه = 'from that place' (پیش‌عبارتی adverbial). تیز modifies لشکر as an adjective/adverb 'swift' or 'rapidly moving'. لشکر is the (understood) object/thing moved, and براند is a third‑person singular present/past narrative form of راندن 'to drive/lead' (here past narrative: 'he drove/led'). The clause thus says he set the army moving from that place; the verb follows the object phrase as is natural in Persian.
segments.s141-g59: کسی را نبد پای با او بجنگ is a negated existential statement: کسی را نبد = 'there was no one' (کسی made definite/indefinite by را, and نبد = 'was not'). پای here is used figuratively 'a foot' = 'an equal/able opponent' and با او بجنگ is an infinitival clause 'to fight with him'. Together: 'There was no one who could stand to fight him.' The verb بجنگ is non‑finite/infinitive-like here ('to fight' / 'fight against'), linked to نبد.
segments.s141-g60: This conditional fragment gives examples: اگر = 'if/when', شیر and پلنگ are nouns ('lion' and 'leopard'). پیش آمدی‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional form meaning roughly 'if (he) came/appeared' (a fused form where آمدی‑گر marks the hypothetical/attendant circumstance). The phrase lists possible opponents: 'if a lion or a leopard appeared before him.'
segments.s141-g61: This is an existential clause: دژی بود 'there was a fortress' (بود = past existential/copula). کش and خواندندی function as name‑forming/qualifying elements here (poetic compound or epithet attached to the fortress) and سپید is an adjective 'white' that qualifies the fortress/name. In classical Persian compounds and epithets often follow the head noun; here the components together give a proper name/description of the fortress.
segments.s141-g62: بران = 'upon that/thence' locates the reference to the fortress just named. دژ repeats 'the fortress' and بد modifies it as 'bad/unfavorable'. ایرانیان را امید places 'the Iranians' as the experiencer/topic (را marks it) and امید is 'hope/prospect'. The whole clause means 'that fortress was a bleak/poor hope for the Iranians' (i.e., a bad prospect from the Iranian viewpoint).
segments.s141-g63: This is a nominal phrase identifying the person: نگهبان دژ 'the guardian of the fortress' followed by رزم دیده, a compound participial adjective 'battle‑seen' (i.e., battle‑hardened/experienced) that modifies the guardian; هجیر is the proper name. The modifier دیده (past participle) follows the noun it qualifies, a common classical Persian order.
segments.s141-g64: Relative clause introduced by که 'who/which' describing Hojir. با زور و دل بود = 'he was with/possessed of strength and heart' (زور = might/force; دل = courage/heart; بود is past copula). و با دار و گیر adds another pair of qualities 'and with دار and گیر' — here دار و گیر together give the sense of 'resources/means and the faculty to seize or hold' (دار and گیر are paired nouns). Grammatically, the sequence is a coordinate predicate listing qualities Hojir possessed.
segments.s141-g73: چو is the archaic/poetic subordinating conjunction 'when'. The clause follows with the subject phrase سهراب جنگ‌آور ('Sohrab, the warrior') followed by the definite object او را ('him' + object marker) and the past verb بدید ('saw'). The object marker را confirms او is the direct, definite object; بدید is a past tense narrative form.
segments.s141-g74: برآشفت is a past verbal form meaning 'became angry' or 'was agitated'. و simply links the two clauses. شمشیر کین is a noun phrase 'sword of vengeance' (noun + genitive/qualifier following it), and برکشید is a past action 'raised' or more specifically here 'drew' (a weapon). Word order is typical: verb after subject/objects in narrative past.
segments.s141-g75: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the host/army'. برون تاخت is a past verbal phrase 'rushed out/forth'. برسان is an imperative/hortative form 'bring' or 'let be brought' (a terse narrative command), and شیر is 'lion' (either the subject that rushed out or the object of the command depending on poetic compression). The phrase shows the telegraphic, performative style of epic narration where an imperative can appear embedded in the report.
segments.s141-g76: به پیش هجیر is 'into the presence of Hejir' (به + پیش = toward/before someone). اندر آمد is the past tense 'came in/entered', and دلیر is an epithet 'the brave' standing after the verb in apposition as the agent — 'the brave came (forward) into Hejir's presence.' The word order (verb then epithet) is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s141-g77: چنین ('thus') modifies گفت ('said'). با رزم‌دیده here marks Hejir with an epithet — 'Hejir, the battle‑scarred' — where با + adjective-noun functions to present the speaker in apposition. The whole segment is the narrative tag introducing direct speech.
segments.s141-g78: که introduces the content of what was said. تنها به جنگ آمدی is a past second‑person statement 'you came alone to battle' (تنها modifies آمدی). خیره is an adjective or vocative 'staring/amazed' addressing the addressee. خیر here is a short exclamatory/colloquial particle appended to the question (an evaluative tag); the whole line is a reproachful question addressed to the combatant.
segments.s141-g79: This is a set of interrogative noun phrases: چه مردی ('what sort of man are you') followed by و نام و نژاد تو ('and your name and lineage'). The clause ends with چی ست (colloquial split of 'چیست' = 'what is'), so the copula is split off as ست; word order is typical for rhetorical questioning in classical Persian.
segments.s141-g80: که here introduces the explanatory or consequential clause 'that ...'. زاینده را is 'the one who gave you life' with the object marker را (the figure being lamented), بر تو ('over you') uses بر = 'over/against' and تو is the person referred to, and باید گریست is an impersonal necessity 'one must weep' or 'it is right to mourn'. The whole means 'that the giver of your life must lament over you.'
segments.s141-g9: سخن زین درازی چه باید کشید is a compact, poetic question. سخن ('speech/words') is the topical noun; زین درازی ('of/from this longness' or 'so long') modifies it (classical ordering: noun + modifier). چه باید کشید literally places the interrogative چه ('what/how/why') before the impersonal modal باید ('must/ought'), with کشید (a past-form of کشیدن) giving the sense 'to draw out / to prolong' — together: 'what reason is there to draw [it] out?' Note that classical poetry often omits an explicit subject; the verb cluster refers back to the speech (i.e. 'to prolong the speech').
segments.s141-g10: هنر برتر از گوهر آمد پدید is an independent clause: هنر ('skill, art') is the subject; برتر از گوهر ('superior to jewels') is a comparative phrase with برتر before the 'from/of' phrase (classical word order). آمد پدید is a two-word verbal sequence meaning 'came into being / appeared' (آمد = came, پدید = appeared/manifested). Together: 'a skill superior to jewels came to light.'
segments.s141-g11: چو افراسیاب آن سخنها شنود is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو ('when'); the proper name افراسیاب is the subject, آن سخنها ('those words') is the object/topic, and شنود (classical 3rd singular past/present form of شنیدن) functions as 'he heard.' Classical poetry often uses the simple-form verb here to narrate past action.
segments.s141-g12: خوش آمدش خندید و شادی نمود is the main clause following the temporal clause. خوش آمدش is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'it pleased him' (خوش = pleasing, آمد + ش enclitic = 'came to him'); the following verbs خندید ('he laughed') and شادی نمود ('he showed/displayed joy') are simple past narrative verbs, coordinated by و. نمود here is a past/transitive form meaning 'displayed/shown'.
segments.s141-g13: ز لشکر گزید از دلاور سران is a compact clause where ز (poetic 'from') and از ('from/of') both mark partitive relations: ز لشکر گزید = 'he chose from the army'; از دلاور سران narrows that selection to 'the brave leaders' (دلاوران 'the brave' with سران 'leaders/heads'). گزید is the past tense 'he chose/selected.' Classical Persian freely arranges these modifiers around the verb.
segments.s141-g14: کسی کاو گراید به گرز گران is a relative/qualifying phrase describing the kind of person chosen. کسی = 'someone'; کاو here is an archaic/clitic relative (classical contraction like 'که او') introducing the subordinate verb گراید ('turns/leans/takes up' — here 'takes up' or 'is inclined to use'); به گرز گران = 'to/with a heavy mace' (گرز گران = 'heavy mace'). The clause therefore specifies 'someone who would take up the heavy mace.' Note: کاو is an archaic form and the printed lemma senses provided do not match its function here.
segments.s141-g15: ده و دو هزار از دلیران گرد is a numeric and partitive noun phrase. ده و دو هزار is the classical way to render 'twelve thousand' (literally 'ten and two thousand'); از دلیران = 'of/among the brave'; گرد here in classical epic usage denotes a troop/group (a unit), so the whole segment names the contingent 'twelve thousand of the brave [troops].' The poetically compact order places the numeral before the partitive phrase.
segments.s141-g16: چو هومان و مر بارمان را سپرد is the verb phrase telling what was done with that force. چو ('then/when') links back to the previous action; هومان و مر بارمان = the recipients (Homan and Barman) — note that the element مر is ambiguous in the provided lemma list and does not cleanly match the classical function here, so it is treated as part of the recipient phrase; را is the definite object marker attached to بارمان in the printed segmentation but grammatically the object of سپرد is the troop mentioned earlier and the recipient is هومان و بارمان. سپرد is the past tense 'entrusted/handed over.' Together: 'Then he entrusted [them] to Homan and Barman.'
segments.s141-g49: به سهراب is an allative prepositional phrase marking the recipient ('to/for Sohrab') placed before the verb. آگاهی آمد is a simple past narrative clause ('information/news came'); آمد is the past of آمدن. The trailing ز راه is an ablative/locative phrase ('from/by the road, by way of') with ز, the poetic equivalent of از.
segments.s141-g50: This segment lists the sources of the news. ز هومان uses the poetic preposition ز ('from') + name Homan; the coordination z/az alternation is stylistic: از بارمان repeats the same idea ('from Barman') and the final و سپاه adds 'and the army' (the last noun is coordinated to the previous items).
segments.s141-g51: پذیره بشد is a short resultative/predicative phrase: پذیره (reception, being received) with بشد (from شدن, here 'was/it came about that he was received' — past/result). با + نیا is an adjunct ('with Niyā'); the role/identity of نیا is uncertain in this line (see senses). همچو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (همچو = 'like', باد = 'wind' here).
segments.s141-g52: سپه دید is a simple past clause 'he saw the army/host' (سپه = host, دید = saw). چندان here modifies the scale/intensity ('so much/to that extent' — i.e. such a vast host), and دلش گشت شاد is a result clause: دلش ('his heart') + گشت (become) + شاد (happy). The possessive ش attaches to دل as the enclitic pronoun 'his'.
segments.s141-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause هومان ورا دید follows the older Persian order where the object pronoun ورا ('him/thither') follows the subject. با یال و کفت is an instrumental/qualifying phrase describing how he was seen ('with mane and foam'), i.e. the horse/person appearing fierce or foaming (کفت here = foam).
segments.s141-g54: فروماند is a past/result verb meaning 'was overwhelmed/overcome/struck down' (an intransitive stative). The following هومان is the subject (repeated for emphasis), ازو gives the cause/agentive source ('by/from him'), and در شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in astonishment/in wonder' describing Homan's state.
segments.s141-g55: بدو is the enclitic allative pronoun 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb. داد is the past 'gave'. پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards' modifying the act. نامه شهریار is a genitival phrase 'the king's letter' (نامه governed by شهریار).
segments.s141-g56: ابا here is used as a verb meaning 'to refuse / be reluctant' (so 'he refused'). What follows is a coordinated list of items refused: هدیه و اسپ و استر ('the gift and the horse and the ast(er)'); به بار is a purpose/destination phrase 'for the baggage/as load' (i.e. he would not take them as baggage). Note that استر in the lexical list appears only with the proper-name sense; in this context it is an animal (mare/pack-animal), so that sense is not present in the provided lemma senses.
segments.s141-g97: This clause contains a sequence of past actions. ز اسپ اندر آمد is a classical/perhaps somewhat archaic phrasing meaning 'came off/from the horse' (اندر آمد used with از to mean 'dismounted'). نشست is the simple past 'sat (down)'. از here introduces the phrase that follows ('from/at') and combines with بر + the enclitic ـش to indicate 'by/at his side' (i.e. 'sat down beside him').
segments.s141-g98: همی خواست is a progressive/past construction: همی marks ongoing or attempted action and خواست is the past 'wanted/attempted', so together 'he was about/attempted to'. از تن بریدن is an infinitival complement (from the body, i.e. sever off from the body) where بریدن is the infinitive 'to cut/sever' used here in the sense 'to behead'. سرش is head + enclitic ـش 'his' (object of بریدن).
segments.s141-g99: بپیچید (past) 'he turned/veered' and و links the next verb; برگشت (past) 'he returned/turned back'. بر + دست راست forms a locative phrase 'onto/to the right hand/side' (بر indicating direction/position and راست specifying 'to the right'). The two verbs are sequential past actions.
segments.s141-g100: غمی شد is a light‑verb construction meaning 'sorrow arose / he was stricken with grief' (غمی 'a grief' + شد 'became'). ز here introduces the experiencer/source ('from/for Sohrab' — i.e. Sohrab was the one afflicted). و links the next phrase; زنهار خواست uses زنهار (an interjection/admonition often used as a plea or warning) as the object of خواست 'he begged/asked for' (asked for mercy/warning).
segments.s141-g101: رها کرد is a transitive past 'he released/let go'. ازو چنگ literally 'from him (ازو) [the] grip/claw (چنگ)' — i.e. 'let go of his hold on him'. و joins the second action; زنهار داد 'gave a znehār' = 'gave a warning/uttered an admonition', with داد as the past of 'to give' taking زنهار as its object.
segments.s141-g102: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; خشنود شد is 'became pleased' (خشنود adjective + شد 'became'). پند بسیار داد is a simple clause 'he gave much advice' where پند 'advice' is the direct object, بسیار modifies it 'many/much', and داد is the past 'gave'.
segments.s141-g103: ببستش is past 'he bound/tied him' (ببست + enclitic ـش). ببند is an imperative 'bind/tie!' (a command to an attendant). آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon', and رزمجوی is a noun 'the warrior/combatant' that introduces who acts or who is relevant in the next clause. The sequence mixes narrator's report and direct imperative.
segments.s141-g104: به نزدیک هومان is a prepositional phrase 'to the vicinity/near Hūmān' (به = to/toward, نزدیک = near, هومان a proper name). فرستاد اوی is the verb 'sent' (فرستاد) with the clitic اوی 'him' as object — 'sent him'. Together: 'sent him to near Hūmān.'
segments.s141-g33: یکی before a noun is an archaic/poetic way to mean “a certain” (not the numeral). نامه is the noun “letter.” با + لابه و دلپسند is an adverbial/qualifying phrase: با + لابه (an old/poetic word meaning pleading/entreaty; here used as manner) و دلپسند (“pleasant/pleasing”) together describe the tone or content of the letter. The full noun phrase sets up what was written; the main verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s141-g34: نبشته here functions as a past participle / verbal adjective meaning “written” or a nominal ‘written text’ and serves as the predicate for the preceding noun phrase (i.e. “a letter was written” / “he wrote a letter”). به نزدیک is an older locative/benefactive phrase meaning “to the presence of / toward that (person) nearby.” آن ارجمند is the definite respectful phrase “that esteemed one,” the addressee.
segments.s141-g35: که‌گر is a conditional particle meaning “if.” تخت ایران is the direct object “the throne of Iran.” به + چنگ آوری is a light-verb construction: به چنگ (into grasp) + آوری (you bring/you take) — literally “bring into (your) hand/grasp.” The verb آوری is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive/optative in classical style, completing the conditional clause.
segments.s141-g36: زمانه is personified here (often meaning Fortune/fortune of the times). برآساید is a classical-form verb meaning “be eased/appeased” (3rd singular optative/present); together they mean “Fortune will be eased.” از introduces the cause/thing from which it is eased (“from judgment”), and داوری is that ablative complement “judgment.”
segments.s141-g37: ازین is an older/poetic spelling of از این (“from this”), where the ین element marks ‘this.’ مرز is “border.” تا آن = “to that.” بسی here functions adverbially (classical use) meaning ‘much’/‘great’ (i.e. ‘much of a road/distance’), and راه نیست is an existential negative: “there is not (much) way/distance.” The clause thus states that the distance between these borders is small.
segments.s141-g38: A simple nominal sentence listing three places joined by و: “Samangan and Iran and Turan.” یکی‌ست (“is one”) is the predicate phrase meaning they are (practically) one/unified or not far apart; note the classical third-person singular copular form.
segments.s141-g39: فرستمت is 1st-person past/perfect “I sent you” (or “I have sent you”). هرچند introduces a concessive sense “although/though.” باید is a modal/impersonal “it is necessary / one must,” and سپاه (“army”) is the noun complement; the clause is elliptical: “though an army is necessary.”
segments.s141-g40: تو is the explicit subject “you.” بر تخت بنشین is an imperative: بر (upon) + تخت (throne) + بنشین (sit down / take your seat on), a direct command. و links the next verbless/nominal phrase برنه کلاه, where برنه is an archaic adjective “bare/without” used predicatively with کلاه (“hat”) — literally “(be) without a hat” or “bare-headed.” The exact pragmatic sense can be ambiguous (it can mean ‘be bare-headed’ or be read idiomatically), but grammatically this is an imperative instruction followed by a descriptive predicate about headgear.
segments.s141-g89: چنان is an adverb meaning 'so/thus' that modifies the whole following clause. نیزه بر نیزه is a poetic juxtaposition 'spear upon spear' (preposition بر = 'on/upon' placed between the nouns), and برساختند is the simple past, 3rd person plural ('they erected/raised/made'); together they form a single verbal phrase describing the action they performed.
segments.s141-g90: که introduces a result/consequence clause ('so that/that'). از یکدگر is the prepositional phrase 'from one another' (از = 'from', یکدگر = 'one another'). بازنشناختند is past tense 3rd plural of the verb 'to recognize' (باز‑نشاختن), used here idiomatically in the result clause ('they did not recognize/failed to recognize one another' in context).
segments.s141-g91: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'one/someone (one of them)'. نیزه زد is a compact past-tense clause 'struck/thrust a spear' (زد = past of زدن). بر میانش is the prepositional/locative phrase 'at/into his middle' (بر here ≈ 'against/at', میانش = میان + enclitic ـش 'his' = 'his middle'). هجیر is a proper name, the target of the action.
segments.s141-g92: نیامد is negative past 'did not come' and here functions idiomatically as 'did not lodge/enter' (i.e., the point did not go in). سنان is the spear (the point/head) as the grammatical subject of this predicate. اندرو is a fused form of اندر + او = 'in him/it'. جایگیر is an adjective/participle 'placed/settled/fit' describing that the spear‑head did not take hold.
segments.s141-g93: سنان is the subject (the spear or its name). باز پس کرد is a compound verbal expression 'pushed back / repelled again' — باز 'again', پس 'back' (here part of the compound), کرد past 'did/made'. The following nouns 'سهراب شیر' are in apposition: 'Sohrab, the lion' (epithet 'lion' modifying Sohrab).
segments.s141-g94: بن here means the 'base/butt' (the lower end) of the spear. نیزه زد is again 'struck/struck with the spear' (زد = past of زدن). بر میان is the locative 'at/into the middle (of)', and دلیر is used substantively 'the brave one' (an epithet for the man struck). The clause describes that the butt, not the point, hit the brave man's middle.
segments.s141-g95: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. زین is 'saddle'. برگرفتش is the past tense stem برگرفت 'took up/lifted' plus the clitic ـش = 'him/it' (i.e., 'he lifted him'). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/behavior of' (به = 'in/with', کرداُر = 'manner/deed'). باد here is simile 'like the wind' ('as if a wind'), giving a swift, airy quality to the action.
segments.s141-g96: نیامد is negative past 'did not come' used impersonally ('no ... came'). همی is an archaic/adverbial particle meaning 'ever/at all' or 'still' emphasizing continuity. زو = ز + او 'from him/it'. بدلش 'his replacement/substitute' (or 'change of state' of him). ایچ 'nothing/not at all' strengthens the negation. یاد here means 'remembrance/trace' — together: 'no trace/remembrance of his change ever came from him.'
segments.s141-g81: هجیرش is the name هجیر with the enclitic third-person object pronoun -ش ('to him' / 'him'); داد پاسخ is an inverted poetic order of the normal گفتن/پاسخ دادن ('gave an answer' = 'replied'); که introduces the reported speech/quote; بس is an interjection meaning 'Enough' or 'that is enough'.
segments.s141-g82: به ترکی is a prepositional phrase ('to / toward a Turk' or 'as regards a Turk'); نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'must not / should not' expressing prohibition or strong refusal; م is a clipped/ectopic token here corresponding to the first‑person object ('me') but the input senses do not include a matching sense (so its sense is not represented); را is the definite/object marker attached to the following object; یار کس literally 'companion/ally of anyone' — together the clause means 'I must not be anyone's ally (to a Turk)'.
segments.s141-g83: This is a nominal copular statement with the name first: هجیر (Hejir) followed by attributive دلیر ('brave') and title سپهبد ('spahbed'); منم is the classical/emphatic copula 'I am' (من + ـم fused), giving 'I am Hejir, brave and spahbed.'
segments.s141-g84: سرت را is the object 'your head' plus the object marker را; هم‌اکنون ('right now') is an adverb of time placed before the verb for emphasis; ز (poetic variant of از) means 'from'; تن 'body' is the source from which the head will be removed; برکنم is the present-subjunctive form used to express intent/future ('I will pluck off / sever'), so the clause reads 'I will right now sever your head from your body.'
segments.s141-g85: فرستم is the first-person present/subjunctive of 'to send' used here to state intent ('I will send / I will dispatch'); به نزدیک = 'to the presence of' (نزدیک used as 'near/presence') and شاه جهان is a compound title 'king of the world' (شاه + جهان). The verb stands before the destination phrase by poetic inversion.
segments.s141-g86: تنت را is 'your body' with the object marker را; کنم ('I will do/make') here functions as 'I will put/lay' (causative/performative sense) and together with the following prepositional phrase expresses disposition of the body; زیر گل = 'under the clay/earth' (گل = clay/earth in this context) and در نهان = 'in secret/hiddenly'. Altogether: 'I will place/bury your body beneath the earth in secret.'
segments.s141-g87: بخندید is the past tense 'he laughed' (verb before subject as a common poetic order); سهراب is the subject following the verb; کاین is a fused relative/ demonstrative ('that/this') and introduces the following noun گفت‌وگوی ('conversation/talk').
segments.s141-g88: به گوش آمدش is a phrase meaning 'it came to his ear' (آمدش = 'came to him'); تیز بنهاد روی is a compact classical expression: تیز ('sharp/keen') + بنهاد (past of بنهادن 'to place/put' – here 'he put/assumed') + روی ('face' or 'countenance'), so the clause means 'he put on/assumed a sharp look' or 'he set his face sharply' (i.e., became intent/serious/attentive).

sentences.s141-s13: After that, bind Sohrab and put him to sleep for one night.
sentences.s141-s14: The two champions rose awake and went toward Sohrab, the bright‑souled.
sentences.s141-s15: They brought before the inner court the king's gift: ten horses and ten mares, with saddles and with loads.
sentences.s141-s16: A throne set with turquoise and a crown from Bi‑jadeh; the crown's top was gold, the throne's pedestal ivory.
sentences.s141-s21: In Turan, there was, without doubt, no commander as brave as Homan or Barman.
sentences.s141-s22: I have now sent (them) at your command so that some may be your guests.
sentences.s141-s23: If you seek battle, they will bring (on) war; they will make the world narrow for the ill‑intentioned.
sentences.s141-s24: They carried off such a letter and the king's robe, accompanied by music and so many horsemen.
sentences.s141-s1: Word reached Afrasiab that Sohrab had launched a boat upon the water.
sentences.s141-s2: Still the scent of milk comes from his mouth; the thought of sword and arrow comes to him.
sentences.s141-s3: The earth will be washed with daggers; now Kavus seeks battle.
sentences.s141-s4: The army gathered in council and went forth against many; no one remembers him.
sentences.s141-s33: At that time Gostaham was a man of wisdom, inclined toward wisdom and full of restlessness/energy.
sentences.s141-s34: One of them was his sister: a mounted rider, ill‑disposed, headstrong, and famed.
sentences.s141-s35: When Sohrab came near the fortress, the valiant Hajir saw the host.
sentences.s141-s36: He mounted the swift‑footed steed and, like a whirlwind, left the fortress, speeding toward the plain of battle.
sentences.s141-s53: When those at the fortress-gate were informed about Hojir that they had seized him and carried him off captive.
sentences.s141-s54: A tumult arose and the men and women wailed, for Hojir had been diminished in that assembly.
sentences.s141-s9: He told the commander of the troop (the army’s commander) that this secret must be kept hidden.
sentences.s141-s10: When the two turned their faces toward one another, Tahmten was, without doubt, seeking a remedy.
sentences.s141-s11: The father must not be made to know that the son has bound his heart and life to the father's affection.
sentences.s141-s12: Perhaps that brave man — so it is said — when he grows old will be slain by the hand of this champion.
sentences.s141-s29: When Jahān‑juy read the king's letter, he swiftly put his army in motion from that place.
sentences.s141-s30: There was no one who could stand and fight with him — whether a lion or a leopard stepped forward.
sentences.s141-s31: There stood a fortress called (or known as) 'Sepīd‑kesh'; that fortress was a poor hope for the Iranians.
sentences.s141-s32: The fortress's keeper, battle‑seasoned Hojir, who had strength and heart and also had means and seizing‑power.
sentences.s141-s37: When Sohrab the warrior saw him, he grew angry and drew the sword of vengeance.
sentences.s141-s38: From the host a lion rushed forth; bring the lion forward — the brave came into Hejir's presence.
sentences.s141-s39: Thus spoke Hejir, battle‑scarred: 'Why have you come alone to the fight, you who stand amazed?'
sentences.s141-s40: What sort of man are you, and what is your name and lineage, that one who gave you life must weep for you?
sentences.s141-s5: Why drag out such a long speech? A skill superior to jewels appeared.
sentences.s141-s6: When Afrasiab heard those words, he was pleased, laughed, and showed joy.
sentences.s141-s7: From the army he chose leaders of the brave; one who would take up the heavy mace.
sentences.s141-s8: He entrusted twelve thousand of the brave to Homan and Barman.
sentences.s141-s25: News reached Sohrab by the road — from Homan and from Barman and the army.
sentences.s141-s26: He was received (welcomed) — with Niyā like the wind; when he saw the host, his heart became glad.
sentences.s141-s27: When Homan saw him — with mane and foam — Homan was overwhelmed and stood in wonder at him.
sentences.s141-s28: Then he gave him the king's letter; he declined the gifts — the horse and the mare — as baggage.
sentences.s141-s49: He dismounted from the horse, sat down by him, and tried to cut off his head from his body.
sentences.s141-s50: He turned and came back to the right-hand side; grief came upon Sohrab, and he begged for mercy.
sentences.s141-s51: He let go of his grip on him and gave a warning; when he became pleased he gave much advice.
sentences.s141-s52: He had him bound — 'Bind him!' — then he sent him to Hūmān's side.
sentences.s141-s17: He wrote a certain letter, full of entreaty and pleasing words, addressed to that esteemed person nearby.
sentences.s141-s18: If you take the throne of Iran into your grasp, Fortune will be eased of judgment.
sentences.s141-s19: From this border to that there is not much distance; Samangan, Iran, and Turan are one.
sentences.s141-s20: I have sent you; though an army is needed, sit upon the throne and (be) without a hat.
sentences.s141-s45: They set spear upon spear so densely that they could no longer recognize one another.
sentences.s141-s46: One thrust a spear at Hejir's midsection; the spear‑head did not lodge into him.
sentences.s141-s47: Sanan (the spear) drove back Sohrab the lion; the butt of the spear struck the brave man's midriff.
sentences.s141-s48: He lifted him from the saddle like the wind; no trace of his change came to him at all.
sentences.s141-s41: Hejir answered him thus: "Enough! I must not be an ally to any Turk."
sentences.s141-s42: I am Hejir, brave and spahbed; I will right now sever your head from your body.
sentences.s141-s43: I will send (you) before the king of the world; I will hide your body beneath the earth in secret.
sentences.s141-s44: Sohrab laughed; when this conversation reached his ear, he set his face sharply.
